questions.prompt: Why does `let v: Vec<i32> = Vec::new();` need a type annotation, while `let v = vec![1, 2, 3];` does not?

options.A: The `vec!` macro can only ever create vectors of `i32`, so no annotation is possible
options.B: `Vec::new()` provides no values, so the compiler cannot infer the element type; `vec![1, 2, 3]` lets it infer `i32` from the initial values
options.C: `Vec::new()` requires an annotation because vectors store heterogeneous types by default
options.D: The annotation is never actually required; it is purely a style convention

questions.explanation: `Vec<T>` is generic, and with an empty `Vec::new()` there are no elements from which to infer `T`. With `vec![1, 2, 3]`, the compiler infers `Vec<i32>` (the default integer type) from the values.

questions.prompt: Given `let v = vec![1, 2, 3, 4, 5];`, what is the difference between evaluating `&v[100]` and `v.get(100)`?

options.A: Both cause a panic at runtime
options.C: `&v[100]` is a compile-time error because the compiler can see the index is out of range
options.D: `v.get(100)` panics at runtime; `&v[100]` returns `None`

questions.explanation: Indexing with `[]` panics on an out-of-bounds index, while `get` returns an `Option<&T>` (`None` when out of range), letting you handle the missing element gracefully.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile and run this program?

options.B: It compiles but panics at runtime when `push` reallocates the vector
options.C: It fails to compile: `v` cannot be borrowed as mutable because it is also borrowed as immutable (E0502)
options.D: It prints `The first element is: 6`

questions.explanation: `first` is an immutable borrow of `v` that is still used after `v.push(6)`, which needs a mutable borrow. The borrow checker rejects this because `push` might reallocate the vector's memory and invalidate `first`.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.C: It fails to compile: cannot modify `v` while iterating over it

questions.explanation: Iterating with `&mut v` yields mutable references to each element; the `*` dereference operator lets the loop add 50 to each value in place. Modifying element values through the loop's own mutable borrow is allowed.

questions.prompt: A vector can only store elements of a single type. How does the book suggest storing an `i32`, an `f64`, and a `String` in the same vector?

options.A: Declare it as `Vec<i32, f64, String>` with multiple type parameters
options.B: Define an enum whose variants hold each type and store enum values, since all variants are the same enum type
options.C: Vectors automatically box mixed values, so you can push different types directly
options.D: It is impossible to represent values of different kinds in one vector in any form

questions.explanation: An enum like `SpreadsheetCell { Int(i32), Float(f64), Text(String) }` gives every element the same type — the enum — while each variant carries different data. Trait objects cover cases where the set of types isn't known at compile time.

questions.prompt: What happens to a vector's elements when the vector goes out of scope?

options.A: Only the vector itself is freed; heap-allocated elements like `String`s must be freed manually
options.B: The vector is freed and all of its contents are dropped as well
options.C: The elements leak until the program exits
options.D: The elements are dropped only if they implement the `Copy` trait

questions.explanation: Like any struct, a vector is freed when it goes out of scope, and dropping it also drops all of its elements. The borrow checker guarantees no references to elements outlive the vector.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.C: It fails to compile: `v[2]` is out of bounds
options.D: It panics at runtime: index out of bounds

questions.explanation: After `push(3)` the vector is `[1, 2, 3]`, so `v[1]` is 2 and `v[2]` is 3, printing 5. Out-of-bounds indexing is a runtime check, not a compile-time one — and here both indices are valid.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about `Vec<T>` are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Elements are stored next to each other in memory, and `push` may reallocate and move them
options.B: `v.get(i)` returns an `Option<&T>` and never panics for an out-of-range index
options.C: A vector can directly store elements of several different types without any enum or trait-object wrapper
options.D: When a vector is dropped, all of its elements are dropped too
options.E: Indexing with `&v[i]` returns `None` when `i` is out of bounds

questions.explanation: Vectors store same-typed values contiguously on the heap, which is why growing may reallocate (and why holding a reference across a `push` is rejected). `get` returns an `Option`; `[]` indexing panics rather than returning `None`; mixed types require an enum or trait objects.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between `String` and `&str` in Rust?

options.A: `String` is an owned, growable, UTF-8 encoded string; `&str` is a borrowed string slice, which is what string literals are
options.B: `String` is stored on the stack, while `&str` is always stored on the heap
options.C: `String` can hold arbitrary bytes in any encoding, while `&str` must be ASCII
options.D: They are interchangeable aliases for the same type

questions.explanation: `String` is the standard library's owned, growable string type (a wrapper around `Vec<u8>` holding UTF-8), while `str`/`&str` is a borrowed slice view into string data. Both must be valid UTF-8.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile and run this program?

options.A: It fails to compile: `s2` was moved into `push_str` and can no longer be used
options.B: It prints `foobar bar` — `push_str` takes a `&str`, so it does not take ownership of `s2`
options.C: It fails to compile: `push_str` only accepts a `String`, not a string literal
options.D: It prints `foo bar` because `push_str` returns a new string instead of modifying `s1`

questions.explanation: `push_str` has the signature `fn push_str(&mut self, s: &str)`: it borrows its argument rather than taking ownership, so `s2` remains usable afterward, and it appends in place to `s1`.

questions.prompt: What is wrong with this program?

options.B: `&s2` is the wrong type: `+` requires a `String` on the right-hand side, not a reference
options.C: It fails to compile: `s1` was moved by the `+` operator (whose `add` method takes `self`) and cannot be used afterward
options.D: It panics at runtime because `s1` was freed by the concatenation

questions.explanation: The `+` operator calls a method with a signature like `fn add(self, s: &str) -> String`, so it takes ownership of the left operand `s1`; using `s1` afterward is a compile-time "borrow of moved value" error. `&s2` is fine: `&String` deref-coerces to `&str`.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile this program?

options.B: It fails to compile: `String` cannot be indexed by an integer because it does not implement `Index<usize>`
options.C: It compiles but panics at runtime with an index-out-of-bounds error
options.D: It prints `104`, the byte value of `h`

questions.explanation: Rust rejects integer indexing of strings at compile time. Because `String` stores variable-width UTF-8 bytes, `s[0]` would be ambiguous (byte? char?) and could return an invalid fragment of a character, so the `Index<usize>` operation simply isn't implemented.

questions.prompt: Each character in this Cyrillic string takes 2 bytes in UTF-8. What happens when you run this program?

options.B: It fails to compile: ranges cannot be used to slice strings
options.C: It panics at runtime: byte index 1 is not a char boundary
options.D: It prints the first byte of `З` as a number

questions.explanation: Slicing a string by a byte range compiles, but if the range ends in the middle of a multi-byte character, the program panics at runtime — exactly as if an invalid vector index were accessed. `&hello[0..4]` would work and yield `Зд`.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?
questions.explanation: `len` returns the number of bytes, and each of these Cyrillic letters takes 2 bytes in UTF-8, so `len` is 4. `.chars()` iterates over Unicode scalar values, of which there are 2.

questions.prompt: Which statements about building and concatenating strings are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: `push_str` takes a `&str` parameter and does not take ownership of its argument
options.C: The `+` operator takes ownership of its left-hand `String` operand
options.D: `format!` takes ownership of all of its arguments
options.E: `push_str` takes ownership of the string passed to it, so it cannot be used afterward

questions.explanation: `push_str(&mut self, s: &str)` borrows its argument, `push` takes a single `char`, and `+` calls `add(self, s: &str)`, consuming the left operand. `format!` uses references, so none of its arguments are moved — which is one reason the book recommends it for combining multiple strings.

questions.prompt: Which statements about inspecting UTF-8 encoded strings are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: The standard library includes an iterator over grapheme clusters (user-perceived letters)
options.B: `len()` on a `String` returns the number of bytes, not the number of characters
options.C: `.chars()` yields Unicode scalar values, so combining diacritics appear as separate items rather than as part of one letter
options.D: Slicing `&s[0..n]` always succeeds as long as `n <= s.len()`
options.E: `.bytes()` yields the raw `u8` values of the string's UTF-8 encoding

questions.explanation: Strings are `Vec<u8>` wrappers, so `len` counts bytes and `.bytes()` exposes them; `.chars()` gives Unicode scalar values, which don't always match user-perceived letters (e.g., Devanagari diacritics). Grapheme-cluster iteration requires an external crate, and byte-range slicing panics if a boundary falls mid-character.

questions.prompt: What must you do before you can use `HashMap` in your code?

options.A: Nothing — `HashMap` is included in the prelude like `Vec` and `String`
options.B: Bring it into scope with `use std::collections::HashMap;`, since it is not in the prelude
options.C: Add the external `hashmap` crate to `Cargo.toml`
options.D: Import it with `use std::hash::HashMap;`

questions.explanation: Of the three common collections, `HashMap` is the least often used, so it's not brought into scope automatically by the prelude. It lives in `std::collections` and requires a `use` statement (no external crate needed).

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.C: It panics at runtime because the key `Red` is missing
options.D: It fails to compile: `get` returns an `i32`, so `copied` cannot be called on it

questions.explanation: `get` returns `Option<&V>` — here `None` because `"Red"` was never inserted. `.copied()` turns `Option<&i32>` into `Option<i32>`, and `.unwrap_or(0)` supplies the default 0 instead of panicking.

questions.prompt: When you iterate over a hash map with `for (key, value) in &scores`, in what order are the pairs visited?

options.A: In the order the keys were inserted
options.C: In an arbitrary order that may differ from run to run
options.D: Sorted by value, ascending

questions.explanation: A `HashMap` stores entries according to its hashing function, so iteration order is arbitrary and not guaranteed to be stable. If you need ordering, you must sort the entries yourself or use a different structure.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile this program?

options.B: It fails to compile: `field_name` was moved into the map by `insert` and cannot be used afterward
options.C: It fails to compile: hash map keys must be `&str`, not `String`
options.D: It compiles but prints an empty string because the map now owns the data

questions.explanation: For owned types like `String`, `insert` moves both the key and the value into the hash map, which becomes their owner. Using `field_name` afterward is a compile-time "borrow of moved value" error; `Copy` types like `i32` would be copied instead.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.D: It panics at runtime: duplicate key inserted

questions.explanation: Each key in a hash map can have only one value at a time. Calling `insert` with a key that already exists replaces the old value, so only `"Blue": 25` remains.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.C: It fails to compile: `count` is a `&mut i32`, which cannot be incremented

questions.explanation: `entry(word).or_insert(0)` returns a mutable reference (`&mut i32`) to the word's counter, inserting 0 first if the word is new. Dereferencing with `*count += 1` increments it, and `"world"` appears twice in the text.

questions.prompt: After this code runs, what value is associated with the key `Blue`?

options.B: 10, because `or_insert` only inserts when the key has no value; `Blue` already has one
options.C: 60, because `or_insert` adds to the existing value
options.D: The program fails to compile because `entry` cannot be called on a key that already exists

questions.explanation: The `entry` API's `or_insert` inserts the given value only if the key is vacant and otherwise leaves the existing value untouched, returning a mutable reference to whichever value is there. So `Blue` stays 10 while `Yellow` gets 50.

questions.prompt: Which statements about `HashMap<K, V>` are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Calling `insert` with a key that already exists keeps the old value unchanged
options.B: `get` returns an `Option<&V>` rather than the value itself
options.C: Iterating over `&map` visits key/value pairs in an arbitrary order
options.D: Owned `String` keys and values are moved into the map when inserted
options.E: The default SipHash hasher was chosen because it is the fastest hashing algorithm available

questions.explanation: `get` yields `Option<&V>`, iteration order is arbitrary, and the map takes ownership of owned keys and values. `insert` on an existing key overwrites the old value, and SipHash was chosen for resistance to hash-flooding DoS attacks, trading away some speed (you can swap hashers via `BuildHasher`).
